title: The Concept of Law
rationale: The foundational text of modern legal positivism. Hart's union of primary and secondary rules, the rule of recognition, and the internal point of view redefined analytical jurisprudence in the 20th century. The 2012 OUP 3rd edition ISBN 9780199644704 covers xxx + 318pp.
excerpt: The most influential work of legal philosophy in the 20th century, redefining law as a system of primary and secondary rules and reshaping how we understand legal obligation.
keyIdeas: Law as a union of primary and secondary rules
The rule of recognition as the foundation of legal validity
The internal and external aspects of legal rules
The separation of law and morality
keyTakeaways: Law is best understood as a system of primary rules (duties) and secondary rules (power-conferring)
The rule of recognition is the ultimate criterion of legal validity in any legal system
Legal obligation involves the internal point of view — accepting rules as guiding behavior
Law and morality are conceptually distinct, though they may overlap in content
whoShouldRead: Philosophers of law and political theorists
Anyone interested in what law is and how it functions
Judges and legal practitioners seeking theoretical foundations
whoShouldSkip: Those seeking practical legal advice or case law
Readers without interest in abstract legal theory
People looking for a critique of specific legal systems
